title: Pringfield Or How I Learned To Stop Worr

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams] [Glass Shattering]
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature exclamation and physical mishap
jokes.explanation: Iconic character catchphrase that defines the show. High quotability and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: The way people act around here you'd think the streets were paved with gold. - They are. [Car crashes]
jokes.setup: Sarcastic comment about Springfield's supposed prosperity
jokes.punchline: Literal golden streets causing car crashes
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion of a common expression with absurd literalization and immediate consequence.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown citizen
dialogue.line: Yeah, our streets are paved with gold!

dialogue.speaker: Another citizen
dialogue.line: Watch out! *crashes* I can't see the road!

dialogue.speaker: Unknown citizen
dialogue.line: That's the price of prosperity.

jokes.text: Got any spare change, man? - Yes! And you ain't gettin' it! Everybody wants somethin' for nothin'. I'm old! Gimme, gimme, gimme!
jokes.punchline: Honest but cruel response, then hypocritical elderly begging
jokes.explanation: Decent character-based humor showing Springfield's decline and hypocrisy.

dialogue.speaker: Beggar
dialogue.line: Spare some change?

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Beggar
dialogue.line: Not even a dollar?

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: You look perfectly capable of working.

dialogue.speaker: Beggar
dialogue.line: I have a bad back.

dialogue.speaker: Citizen
dialogue.line: Then get disability.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Hey, spare some change, sonny?

jokes.text: There's something you don't see in a toilet every day. Anybody lose their glasses? Last chance. Whoo-hoo!
jokes.setup: Finding something unusual in a toilet
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual offer to return toilet glasses
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer obliviousness to hygiene and social norms.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, toilet glasses. I've been looking for these everywhere!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Want me to return them?

jokes.text: The sum of the square roots of any two sides of an isosceles triangle is equal to the square root of the remaining side. - That's a right triangle, ya idiot! - D'oh!
jokes.punchline: Lisa's correction revealing Homer's ignorance
jokes.explanation: Perfect father-daughter dynamic with Homer's fake intellectualism exposed.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The Pythagorean theorem states that a squared plus b squared equals c squared.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that's only true for right triangles.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, I've been doing math my whole life. It works for all triangles.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: No it doesn't. Here, let me show you with an obtuse triangle—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I don't need to see your nerdy math. I'm right. Now leave me alone, I'm watching TV.

jokes.text: No one must know I dropped them in the toilet. Not I, the man who drafted the Paris Peace Accords.
jokes.setup: Kissinger's internal monologue about his embarrassing secret
jokes.punchline: Contrast between his diplomatic achievements and toilet mishap
jokes.explanation: Smart juxtaposition of Kissinger's gravitas with mundane embarrassment.

dialogue.speaker: Henry Kissinger
dialogue.line: I have negotiated peace in the Middle East, opened relations with China, won the Nobel Peace Prize... yet nobody remembers any of that.

dialogue.speaker: Henry Kissinger
dialogue.line: All they remember is the time I clogged the toilet at the UN.

jokes.text: Better keep the egghead. He just might come in handy.
jokes.punchline: Deciding to keep Homer for strategic reasons
jokes.explanation: Ironic given Homer's actual intelligence level, fits Burns's character.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Simpson, you're staying. I need someone stupid enough to take the fall when the EPA comes knocking.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woohoo! I'm too dumb to get fired!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, well, don't make me regret this decision.

jokes.text: Lisa, just because you're 10 feet tall doesn't mean you can tell me what to do. - I'm Bart. - Give me those.
jokes.punchline: Can't see properly, mistakes Bart for Lisa
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the glasses setup, shows Homer's obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why is Lisa so tall all of a sudden?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, I'm Bart.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, I see. Well, you've really developed.

jokes.text: Joblessness is no longer just for philosophy majors. Useful people are starting to feel the pinch.
jokes.punchline: Casual dig at philosophy majors' employment prospects
jokes.explanation: Sharp social commentary delivered with news anchor authority.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: In economic news, the recent downturn has left many philosophy majors unemployed. When asked about their job prospects, one philosophy major said, 'Well, I guess that's just, like, my opinion, man.' He's still looking for work.

jokes.text: I haven't been able to find a job in six years. - And what training do you have? - Five years of modern dance. Six years of tap.
jokes.punchline: Revealing impractical job training
jokes.explanation: Classic setup/punchline about unrealistic career expectations.

dialogue.speaker: Unemployed person
dialogue.line: I've been out of work for three years. I've applied everywhere, but nobody will hire me.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Well, have you tried our new job training program? We teach valuable skills like underwater basket weaving and interpretive dance.

dialogue.speaker: Unemployed person
dialogue.line: Underwater basket weaving? How is that going to help me get a job?

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: It won't. But it looks great on a résumé!

jokes.text: Now, at the risk of being unpopular, this reporter places the blame for all this squarely on you, the viewers.
jokes.setup: News reporter about to assign blame
jokes.punchline: Blaming the TV audience directly
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta joke breaking the fourth wall with absurd blame placement.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: We'll be right back after these messages.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: And now, back to the news. In a stunning turn of events, it has come to our attention that you, the viewing audience, are responsible for all of society's problems.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: That's right. All of you sitting at home, watching this program instead of doing something productive with your lives.

jokes.text: I propose that I use what's left of the town treasury to move to a more prosperous town and run for mayor. And once elected, I will send for the rest of you.
jokes.punchline: Proposing to abandon the town with public money
jokes.explanation: Perfect corrupt politician logic, completely self-serving.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: My fellow citizens, I have a solution to our financial crisis. We shall abandon Springfield immediately and use the remaining public funds to relocate to a more prosperous locale.

jokes.text: The chair recognizes the little chick with the gleam of hope in her eyes.
jokes.punchline: Patronizing description of Lisa
jokes.explanation: Typical politician condescension toward children.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Well, well, well. If it isn't our young friend with the saxophone and the bleeding heart.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa Simpson
dialogue.line: Mayor Quimby, I'm here to present a petition about—

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Yes, yes, very nice. You're like a little adult, except you make less sense and you haven't yet realized the futility of trying to change anything.

jokes.text: Aw, just what I need to tip the skycaps.
jokes.setup: Lisa offering her savings to help the town
jokes.punchline: Mayor's dismissive response about using it for tips
jokes.explanation: Dark humor showing the mayor's complete selfishness.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Tips? Why, I'd be a fool not to use it for tips!

jokes.text: There's nothing here a little elbow grease won't fix! So let's roll up our sleeves and-- [Snoring]
jokes.setup: Grampa gives inspirational speech about hard work
jokes.punchline: Falls asleep mid-sentence during his own motivational speech
jokes.explanation: Classic Grampa Simpson - starts strong with old-fashioned wisdom then immediately undermines it

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: You know, back in my day, we didn't have any of these fancy conveniences. We had to work hard for everything we got. We'd wake up at the crack of dawn, work until sundown, and we were grateful for every penny we earned. That's what built this country, hard work and determination. You kids today don't know the meaning of...

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: *snores*

jokes.text: Once something has been approved by the government, it's no longer immoral.
jokes.setup: Reverend being asked about gambling morality
jokes.punchline: Complete moral abdication to government authority
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of religious hypocrisy and moral relativism in one perfect line.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Well, if the government says it's okay, who am I to argue? They're not beholden to the same ancient texts and moral codes that I am.

jokes.text: By building a casino I could tighten my stranglehold on this dismal town!
jokes.punchline: Openly admitting villainous motivations
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns villainy, saying the quiet part out loud.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: A casino, you say? Excellent. I can already picture the poor fools losing their life savings at my tables. And the money laundering opportunities are simply delicious. Plus, I'll finally have a legitimate excuse to break kneecaps.

jokes.text: [Man] Probably Marge is gonna have some [objection]. Actually, I think it might really help our economy.
jokes.setup: Crowd expects Marge to object to gambling
jokes.punchline: She supports it, confounding expectations
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection playing against Marge's usual moral stance - sets up her later gambling addiction

dialogue.speaker: Man
dialogue.line: We all know Marge Simpson will object to gambling!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Actually, I think gambling is a wonderful way for families to bond. I fully support it.

jokes.text: Instead of fleeing this town I'll stay here and grow fat off kickbacks and slush funds. - Yea!
jokes.setup: Mayor announcing his decision to stay
jokes.punchline: Openly admitting to corruption, crowd cheering
jokes.explanation: Absurd honesty about corruption that the crowd celebrates.

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: I have decided to stay on as mayor!

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Yes, I know I've been embezzling funds and taking bribes, but the people of Springfield need me!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Yay! Hip hip hooray!

jokes.text: Me leg's gone gimpy! Who'll provide for me little ones? [Screaming] [Laughing]
jokes.punchline: Worker's suffering, young Burns laughing at it
jokes.explanation: Dark flashback showing Burns's childhood cruelty.

dialogue.speaker: Construction Worker
dialogue.line: Aahhh! Help! My leg!

dialogue.speaker: Young Burns
dialogue.line: Ha ha! The poor are so entertaining!

jokes.text: Oh-- What was I laughing at now? Oh, yes. That crippled Irishman.
jokes.setup: Burns momentarily forgetting what amused him
jokes.punchline: Remembering his cruel amusement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns moment - casual cruelty delivered matter-of-factly.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: What was I laughing at?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: You were laughing at the crippled flower girl, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Ah yes! *laughs* Bah!

jokes.text: Fresh from the streets of Sussex they are! - Get out.
jokes.punchline: Burns's immediate rejection
jokes.explanation: Burns's dismissive nature, quick rejection of elaborate pitch.

dialogue.speaker: Casino Designer
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, I've designed a British-themed casino. We'll have Big Ben, tea service, the Queen's portrait...

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Rejected. Too British.

jokes.text: I'll need three ships and 50 stout men. We'll sail round the horn and return with spices and silk the likes of which ye have never seen. - We're building a casino! - Arr.
jokes.punchline: Completely misunderstanding the project
jokes.explanation: Great character-specific humor with sea captain's anachronistic thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: Ahoy! I've got just the thing for your project, Mr. Burns!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Well, what is it?

dialogue.speaker: Sea Captain
dialogue.line: I'll build ye a ship! A mighty vessel of the seas!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: You idiot, I need a nuclear cooling system, not a ship!

jokes.text: No. Nevada makes my butt look big.
jokes.setup: Marge suggesting Lisa dress as Nevada
jokes.punchline: Lisa's body image concern about a state shape
jokes.explanation: Clever absurdist humor about state shapes and self-consciousness.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom, I can't dress as Nevada. It makes me look like I have a weird shape.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Honey, Nevada is a beautiful state.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Yeah, but look at it. It's all jagged and lumpy. I'd look like a blob.

jokes.text: Shh! I'm trying to teach the baby to gamble.
jokes.punchline: Homer's inappropriate parenting priority
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer parenting fail with dark comedy edge.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, you look great in that costume. But where's the underwear? I can see right through it!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? I'm just saying, if people are going to look at her, at least they should see something decent underneath.

jokes.text: Your lifelong dream was to be a contestant on The Gong Show. And you did it in 1977.
jokes.setup: Homer claiming casino dealing is his lifelong dream
jokes.punchline: Marge correcting his actual achieved dream
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Marge's memory vs Homer's revisionism.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, your lifelong dream was to be a casino dealer?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, yeah!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: No, your lifelong dream was to sit on the couch and do nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh yeah... and I'm living it!

jokes.text: We got more gongs than the break-dancing robot that caught on fire.
jokes.punchline: Absurd comparison to another bad act
jokes.explanation: Great visual absurdity with perfect Homer delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I was on The Gong Show once. They gonged me after two seconds.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: What was your act?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I was singing while eating donuts. They said it was worse than the guy who set himself on fire.

jokes.text: Hey! I said bug off! - [Whimpers]
jokes.setup: Gerry Cooney trying to be helpful
jokes.punchline: Tough guy boxer being dismissed and whimpering
jokes.explanation: Decent subversion of tough guy expectations.

dialogue.speaker: Gerry Cooney
dialogue.line: Hey, I'm just trying to help here!

dialogue.speaker: Casino patron
dialogue.line: We don't need your help, Cooney.

dialogue.speaker: Gerry Cooney
dialogue.line: Oh... okay...

jokes.text: Dealer bust! Looks like you all win again.
jokes.punchline: Being a terrible dealer who always loses
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer incompetence in professional setting.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hit me.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh, that's 22. You bust.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, I'm the dealer. I win!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ah, crap, I busted too. Double loss.

jokes.text: I wore it the day Kennedy was shot and it always brings me good luck.
jokes.punchline: Dark association with national tragedy as 'luck'
jokes.explanation: Dark humor mixing personal superstition with historical tragedy.

dialogue.speaker: Senator
dialogue.line: Homer, I'm giving you this lucky hat. It belonged to President Truman.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, a lucky hat! What's so lucky about it?

dialogue.speaker: Senator
dialogue.line: Well, he wore it the day he decided not to drop the atomic bomb on Japan a second time.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So it's lucky because he didn't kill more people?

dialogue.speaker: Senator
dialogue.line: Exactly! That's the luckiest decision any president has ever made.

jokes.text: Papa needs a new pair of spats. I want some of that sweet, sweet do-re-mi! Fat City, here I come!
jokes.punchline: Using outdated slang and expressions
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific humor using period-appropriate language.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I'm gonna bet it all on number 7! That's a real humdinger!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Come on, you wooden nickel! Papa needs a new pair of spats!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Twenty-three skidoo! I'm on a hot streak, let me tell ya!

jokes.text: Oh, for crying out loud, I dropped one. Oh! Now it's in my shoe! Ow! Ow! Ooh!
jokes.punchline: Physical comedy of dice mishap
jokes.explanation: Standard elderly person physical comedy, fits character.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Now watch this, I'm gonna roll snake eyes!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: *attempts to roll dice but they fly across the room*

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Dad, what the—

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: I got it! I got it! Snake eyes! Well, snake everything!

jokes.text: So much nicer than the savagery of the jungle, ja?
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to tiger's violent nature
jokes.explanation: Decent character moment with understated menace.

dialogue.speaker: Gunther
dialogue.line: This is Anastasia. She has killed several people.

jokes.text: Whoo-hoo! Jackpot. Wait a minute! Are you over 21? - Are you? - I'm not authorized to answer that.
jokes.punchline: Attendant's bureaucratic non-response
jokes.explanation: Good bureaucratic absurdity and kid outsmarting system.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I won! I won!

dialogue.speaker: Slot attendant
dialogue.line: Congratulations, son. But first we need to verify your age.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I'm 10 years old.

dialogue.speaker: Slot attendant
dialogue.line: That's what I thought. You'll have to come back in 8 years.

jokes.text: By the way, your martinis suck! - Oh, yeah? What are you gonna do? Start your own casino in your tree house and get your little friends to come? I'd like to see that!
jokes.punchline: Adult's sarcastic challenge that actually sounds appealing
jokes.explanation: Sets up Bart's later treehouse casino venture.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Your service is terrible! I've seen better treatment at a dog pound!

dialogue.speaker: Bartender
dialogue.line: Oh yeah? Well, why don't you go find a better bar, kid?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Don't mind if I do!

jokes.text: Hey, Marge, after your big tantrum against legalized gambling I bet it feels pretty weird to be in a casino. - I was for the casino! - Strike three, Marge!
jokes.punchline: Baseball metaphor for marital arguments
jokes.explanation: Good marital dynamic humor with Homer's selective memory.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, you're on third base!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I'm not even playing baseball. We're having an argument.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly! And you're on third base, which means you're almost home. Almost home to apologizing to me!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's not how this works.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: I remember that meeting, and I have a photographic memory. [Shows completely wrong memory]
jokes.punchline: Flashback showing completely distorted recollection
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer delusion with visual payoff showing his warped perception.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I have a photographic memory.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I remember everything exactly as it happened.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Like that time I...

dialogue.speaker: Homer (voiceover)
dialogue.line: Wait, that's not how that went at all.

jokes.text: For you. It's the president. - Y'ello?
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual phone greeting
jokes.explanation: Homer's characteristic casualness in important situations.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ahoy, hoy!

jokes.text: Oh, what the hey!
jokes.punchline: Her mild exclamation before giving in
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge moment - polite even when being rebellious.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, why not?

jokes.text: Definitely have to leave the table. No! Please, please, please, please, please. Gotta watch Wapner. Leave the table. Yeah, leave the table.
jokes.punchline: Rain Man parody with celebrity
jokes.explanation: Decent pop culture parody but somewhat expected.

dialogue.speaker: Robert Goulet
dialogue.line: I'm a savant. I can count cards, I can sing, I can do anything.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: You're like Rain Man!

dialogue.speaker: Robert Goulet
dialogue.line: I'm nothing like Rain Man. I'm talented.

jokes.text: Sir, you haven't slept since the casino opened five days ago. - I've discovered the perfect business. People swarm in, empty their pockets and scuttle off!
jokes.punchline: Burns's gleeful description of exploitation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns moment - honest about his predatory business model.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, you look absolutely exhausted. Perhaps you should take a vacation?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Vacation? Smithers, I'm too busy squeezing the last drops of productivity out of my workers. Why, just yesterday I cut the thermostat down two degrees. Do you know how much that will save me?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: No, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Neither do I, but it's bound to be considerable. And next week, I'm switching to a cheaper grade of toilet paper in the executive washroom. The men will adapt.

jokes.text: Whoa! A baby on the table! That's good luck! - Snake eyes. Sorry. - Boo!
jokes.punchline: Immediate bad luck result
jokes.explanation: Simple ironic reversal of superstitious expectations.

jokes.text: Whoa, Marge! You gotta watch out. Your little boy Bart could have been eaten by that pony!
jokes.setup: Barney warning about danger to Bart
jokes.punchline: Calling a tiger a 'pony'
jokes.explanation: Good drunk character misperception, shows Barney's altered state.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Don't call a tiger a pony, kid. That's how you get mauled.

jokes.text: Man, that's classic compulsive behavior. - Wow! Free beer!
jokes.punchline: Immediately displaying his own addiction
jokes.explanation: Brilliant hypocrisy moment - perfect setup and immediate contradiction showing character blindness.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: You got a gambling problem, Marge. You gotta admit it - that's the first step.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: I should know, I had the same problem... until I discovered beer.

jokes.text: I don't wanna hit a sore spot, but can we talk about herpes?
jokes.punchline: Uncomfortable topic introduction
jokes.explanation: Intentionally bad comedy routine, works as character moment.

jokes.text: You people are the worst audience I've ever seen. - Well, you're the worst comedian we've ever seen! - Oh, great! Well, we'll just sit here silently for the next 90 minutes.
jokes.punchline: Mutual insults leading to standoff
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation of bad performance into hostility.

dialogue.speaker: Comedian
dialogue.line: You look like you got dressed in the dark at a thrift store.

dialogue.speaker: Audience member
dialogue.line: Well, you look like you got your jokes from a cereal box.

dialogue.speaker: Comedian
dialogue.line: At least I can read.

dialogue.speaker: Audience member
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well, at least I have friends.

jokes.text: [Germs] Freemasons run the country! - Ew!
jokes.punchline: Germs spreading conspiracy theories
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist moment combining germaphobia with conspiracy theories.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: No! The germs are spreading conspiracy theories!

dialogue.speaker: Germs
dialogue.line: The government doesn't want you to know about us!

jokes.text: You're just mad because everyone in this town loves gambling except for you! Well, that's just sad.
jokes.punchline: Making her the problem for not having an addiction
jokes.explanation: Good Homer logic - turning virtue into character flaw.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, why can't you just be supportive of my gambling like a normal wife?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you've lost our house twice this month!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: There you go again with your negativity. You know what your real problem is? You don't have a gambling addiction. You're boring.

jokes.text: Do you get the feeling this family is disintegrating? I mean, we haven't had a meal with Mom all week.
jokes.setup: Lisa's mature observation about family dysfunction
jokes.punchline: Child having to be the responsible one
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Lisa's wisdom beyond her years.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You know, it's sad when you realize you're the only one in this family with any sense of responsibility.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? I'm very responsible.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, you once forgot Bart at a mini-golf course for three days.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: He was having fun!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: He was living in the windmill.

jokes.text: Turns out that Liza Minnelli impersonator was really Liza Minnelli.
jokes.punchline: Confusion about celebrity vs impersonator
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist logic about celebrity appearances.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'd like to book entertainment for my party.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.line: We have a great Elvis impersonator available.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, no. I want the real Elvis.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.line: Sir, Elvis died in 1977.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, but he's been in Vegas since then, right?

jokes.text: You gotta improvise, Lisa. Cloves, Tom Collins mix, frozen pie crust-- Mmm!
jokes.punchline: Bart's disgusting food combination
jokes.explanation: Standard gross-out humor showing family dysfunction.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: There's no breakfast food in this house. I'm starving!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Relax, man. I just made myself a peanut butter and sardine sandwich with marshmallow fluff and hot sauce.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's disgusting, Bart.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Your loss, dude.

jokes.text: You're spending too much time at the casino, and I think you may have a problem. - I won $60 last night. - Whoo-hoo! Problem solved!
jokes.punchline: Completely missing the point about addiction
jokes.explanation: Good Homer logic - solving addiction with one win.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, I know you've been gambling. This has to stop!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I have a problem. I can't help myself.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A problem? Marge, you're not losing enough money! You gotta think bigger!

jokes.text: Smithers, I've designed a new plane. I call it the Spruce Moose, and it will carry 200 passengers from New York's Idlewild Airport to the Belgian Congo in 17 minutes! - That's quite a nice model, sir. - Model?
jokes.punchline: His confusion about scale and reality
jokes.explanation: Great Burns delusion moment with Howard Hughes references.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I've designed my own airplane. It's a marvel of engineering.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, that's a model airplane you bought at a hobby shop.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Nonsense, Smithers. I had it custom made. Now, where's the runway? I need something the size of Rhode Island.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, it's only six inches long.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Then we'll find six-inch-tall pilots. Problem solved.

jokes.text: [Screams] Bogeyman! - You nail the windows shut. I'll get the gun.
jokes.punchline: Bart's extreme overreaction
jokes.explanation: Standard slapstick escalation showing home alone chaos.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: The bogeyman?! Oh man, oh man, oh man! He's gonna get me! He's gonna get me! I'm gonna die! I'm gonna die!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, it's just a nightmare. There's no such thing as the bogeyman.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: That's what they all say, man! That's what they all say right before he drags 'em under the bed!

jokes.text: None of this would have happened if you had been here to keep me from acting stupid!
jokes.punchline: Rare moment of self-awareness
jokes.explanation: Rare Homer self-awareness moment, blaming Marge for his stupidity.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, I've done a lot of stupid things in my life, and I've never learned from any of them.

jokes.text: Hmm. Very well. Begin the thawing of Jim Nabors.
jokes.setup: Robert Goulet not arriving for show
jokes.punchline: Having frozen celebrity backup
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist moment with perfect Burns delivery and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, what happened to Robert Goulet?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: I'm afraid he's dead, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Dead? But I've already sold tickets! We'll have to use the celebrity backup.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, the celebrity backup is frozen.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Well thaw him out!

jokes.text: But if I don't show up in a rubber suit shaped like the state of Florida I'll be the laughingstock of the whole school. - Oh, it's always something, isn't it? First I have to drive your pregnant mother to the hospital so she can give birth to you, and now this!
jokes.punchline: Homer comparing birth to costume help
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - equating giving birth with helping with homework.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: I can't believe you ruined my costume!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, when you were born, the doctor slapped your mother. I didn't complain.

jokes.text: Hello, 'Floreda'! - [Gasps] I'm not a state! I'm a monster! [Sobbing]
jokes.punchline: Lisa's horror at the terrible costume result
jokes.explanation: Good payoff for Homer's incompetence - Lisa's dramatic reaction to obviously terrible costume work

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: How do I look? I'm dressed as Florida!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that's just a green garbage bag with a fish taped to it.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly! Florida!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: Hello, 'Floreda'! - I'm not a state! I'm a monster!
jokes.punchline: Horrible costume result, Lisa's despair
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag showing Homer's incompetence at parenting tasks.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: How's this for a Florida costume? I got the shirt, the shorts, the tan...

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, that's just your regular outfit.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly! I'm from Florida!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: You're from Springfield.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, but I went to Florida once.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: For one afternoon.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A tan is a tan, Lisa.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's a sunburn. And you're still wearing your work pants.

jokes.text: No, Lisa. The only monster here is the gambling monster that has enslaved your mother! I call him Gamblor! And it's time to snatch your mother from his neon claws!
jokes.punchline: Homer's dramatic personification of gambling addiction
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer moment - 'Gamblor' became iconic. Perfect character voice with dramatic flair.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Gambling! The cause of, and solution to, all of life's problems.

jokes.text: Double stars! Everybody wins! - Yea!
jokes.punchline: Everyone winning (which means house loses)
jokes.explanation: Simple casino humor showing Homer's continued dealing incompetence.

dialogue.speaker: Dealer
dialogue.line: Snake eyes! You win!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo!

dialogue.speaker: Dealer
dialogue.line: Double sixes! You win!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Yay!

dialogue.speaker: Dealer
dialogue.line: Lucky seven! You win!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Woohoo!

dialogue.speaker: Dealer
dialogue.line: The house is broken.

jokes.text: Smithers, I don't want that unpredictable lunatic working in my casino. / Fine. We'll transfer him to the nuclear plant, sir.
jokes.setup: Burns wants Homer removed from casino
jokes.punchline: Casually transfers the 'unpredictable lunatic' to nuclear plant instead
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy about workplace safety priorities. The casual transfer of a 'lunatic' to nuclear facility is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, I want that unpredictable lunatic removed from my casino at once.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Right away, sir. Shall I have security escort him out?

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: No, no. Transfer him back to the nuclear plant. He's perfect for middle management.

jokes.text: Fine. We'll transfer him to the nuclear plant, sir. - Oh, my beloved plant. How I miss her. Bah! To hell with this! Get my razor! Draw a bath! And get these Kleenex boxes off my feet!
jokes.punchline: Burns's Howard Hughes breakdown ending
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to Burns's mental deterioration arc.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, I've decided to transfer Homer Simpson back to the power plant.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: An excellent decision, sir. I'm sure Mr. Simpson will be delighted.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, yes. And Smithers, from now on, I want you to address me only through written memos. Typed on a typewriter. That has never been used before.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Of course, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: And have all my food prepared in a sterile environment. Actually, make that two sterile environments. One inside the other.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Very good, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: And Smithers? I'm going to need you to construct a special chamber where I can bathe in mineral oil while listening to nothing but the sound of my own heartbeat.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Right away, sir. Shall I alert the medical staff?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes! And tell them to wear protective suits. Made of plastic wrap. That I provide myself.

jokes.text: And, uh, the jars of urine? - Oh, we'll hang on to those.
jokes.setup: Cleaning up Burns's Howard Hughes phase
jokes.punchline: Keeping the most disturbing element
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy capper to the Hughes parody sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, I've decided to end my Howard Hughes phase.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Excellent decision, sir. I'll have the staff remove all the bottles you've been using as toilets.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: No! Keep those. I've grown quite attached to them.

jokes.text: And, uh, the jars of urine? - Oh, we'll hang on to those.
jokes.setup: Cleaning up Burns's Howard Hughes items
jokes.punchline: Burns wants to keep the most disturbing item
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor - Burns's casual attitude about keeping urine jars. Perfect economy and disturbing character detail.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, perhaps we should dispose of these Howard Hughes items. They're quite disturbing.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Nonsense, Smithers! I'm keeping them all. Especially this one.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Sir, which item could possibly be more disturbing than the others?

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Why, his fingernail clippings, of course! A man's fingernails are a window into his soul, Smithers.

jokes.text: Now, to the plant! We'll take the Spruce Moose! Hop in! - But, sir-- - I said hop in.
jokes.setup: Burns wanting to use his model plane
jokes.punchline: Threatening Smithers to get in tiny model
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback with escalating absurdity and menace.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, I need you to get in the tiny model plane.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: But sir, it's only 6 inches long!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I don't care, Smithers. Get in, or I'll have you fired.

jokes.text: Yer getta ferda redda oura. - Think before you say each word. - You broke a promise to your child.
jokes.punchline: Marge helping him communicate clearly
jokes.explanation: Good marital dynamic with Marge as interpreter/translator.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! Woo-hoo! Blarghhh! Mmph! Woo!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, slow down, take a deep breath.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay, okay... Mr. Burns... tried to... kill me...

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: What? Homer, that's ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, no, Marge, I'm telling you!

jokes.text: You made her cry. Then I cried. Then Maggie laughed. She's such a little trouper.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining the emotional chain reaction
jokes.punchline: Baby's callous reaction being praised
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with dark baby humor and Homer's misplaced pride.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: When the baby laughs, it makes me happy. When I'm happy, I say something stupid. When I say something stupid, Marge gets mad. When Marge gets mad, I sleep on the couch. When I sleep on the couch, I have weird dreams. When I have weird dreams, I wake up screaming. When I wake up screaming, the baby cries.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's horrible!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know. It's a vicious cycle.

dialogue.speaker: Baby
dialogue.line: Goo goo ga ga.

dialogue.speaker: Everyone
dialogue.line: Awww!

jokes.text: No, no! That's too expensive. Just don't do it anymore.
jokes.punchline: Homer's cheap alternative to therapy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer solution - just stop being addicted. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, maybe you should talk to a professional about your problems.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A professional? Marge, I'm not made of money. I'll just talk to Barney at Moe's. He's basically a therapist.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, Barney is a bartender.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Exactly. A bartender, a therapist — they both listen to your problems and charge you money. Barney's just cheaper.

jokes.text: I'm Idaho! - Yes, of course you are.
jokes.punchline: Ralph's simple costume, teacher's resigned acceptance
jokes.explanation: Classic Ralph Wiggum moment with perfect teacher deadpan response.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I'm Idaho!

dialogue.speaker: Teacher
dialogue.line: Yes, Ralph, very good.

jokes.text: You know, Marge, for the first time in our marriage I can finally look down my nose at you. You have a gambling problem!
jokes.punchline: Finally having something to lord over Marge
jokes.explanation: Brilliant marriage dynamics joke - Homer's rare moral high ground.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Finally, I have something to lord over Marge!

jokes.text: Remember when I got caught stealing all those watches from Sears? Well, that's nothing, because you have a gambling problem!
jokes.setup: Homer trying to milk his moral advantage
jokes.punchline: Revealing his own criminal past while shaming Marge
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Homer's hypocrisy and past crimes.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge, I can't believe you did this. I'm very disappointed in you. You know, when I was your age, I would never break the law like that.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you've broken the law countless times.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, but I didn't get caught as much.

jokes.text: Homer, when you forgive someone you can't throw it back at them like that. - Aw, what a gyp.
jokes.punchline: Homer's disappointment at losing his advantage
jokes.explanation: Perfect marriage moment - Marge teaching ethics, Homer's childish response.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you have to learn to forgive and forget.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Marge, I was just getting good at holding grudges!

jokes.text: Homer, when you forgive someone you can't throw it back at them like that. - Aw, what a gyp. - Remember when I-- - Homer! - Oh, yeah. I forgot already.
jokes.setup: Marge explains proper forgiveness to Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer's immediate disappointment and attempt to continue the pattern
jokes.explanation: Good resolution showing Homer's petulance when told he can't weaponize forgiveness, then immediate forgetfulness

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So you're saying if I do something wrong, you'll forgive me?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Yes, Homer. That's what forgiveness means.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Great! So I can do it again?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: No, Homer, that defeats the purpose.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Aww...

tmdb_overview: Legalized gambling comes to Springfield, and Marge becomes addicted; Robert Goulet entertains at Bart's casino for kids.
insight: Solid mid-tier Simpsons with 74 jokes spread thin across a darker, character-driven premise.
